title: NAFTA and the Politics of Labor Transnationalism By Tamara Kay Cambridge University Press. 2011. 310 pages. $84 cloth, $31.99 paper
abstract: How did the formulation and implementation of the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) affect transnational cooperation between North American unions? And why did some unions build strong transnational relationships while others did not? In contrast to the large majority of literature which views neoliberal globalization as resulting in a necessary decline in the power of labor, Tamara Kay argues that the passage of NAFTA served as a catalyst for enhanced labor transnationalism between unions in Canada, the United States and Mexico. Specifically, the creation of new “transnational institutional fields” produced new incentives as well as opportunities for unions to work together. This work is very well researched and establishes an innovative framework for analyzing how social movements operate in transnational space. Kay begins by convincingly demonstrating that increased trade was not, in and of itself, the catalyst for expanded union transnationalism. Although trade between North American countries grew quite quickly throughout the 1970s and 1980s, it was not until the enactment of NAFTA became a real possibility that most unions began to actively cooperate across borders. In the early 1990s, a “transnational trade-negotiating field” emerged in the lead-up to NAFTA passage, and unions began to communicate and cooperate within this newly created space. While different unions and union federations had distinct positions on the passage of NAFTA (notably the official Confederation of Mexican Workers supported it), they were all interested in either opposing outright or trying to influence the content of the final trade agreement. In large part because of transnational union coordination, a labor side agreement was eventually included in NAFTA.
